The next thought does not work: the place air and rain are not set by how far north or how high it is but by the change made by a water moving.  

But the north west side of the land in an easy way has the air and rain that many places have, and a well named one at that.  

The thought has made the reason of air and rain too easy by making it down to two things about where a place is, when in true life the things that set the place air and rain have water moving, the moving air that usually blows, and how close the place is to big water areas.
definition: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, (ii) the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and (iii) the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude.
counterexample: The climate of western Norway is far warmer and wetter than its latitude and altitude alone would predict, owing to the influence of the Gulf Stream. The third condition fails — the climatic conditions are not determined solely by latitude and altitude — yet the region plainly has a climate, and a distinctive one.
missing_condition: Climate is shaped by ocean currents, prevailing winds, and continental position as well as by latitude and altitude; the definition has oversimplified the determining factors.

question: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
expected_answer: One might say that breaking a promise works like this: a person has broken a promise if and only if the person said words that meant he would do a certain thing, the other person trusted that promise, and the first person did not do the thing. This idea fits many cases. A person who sells things promises to bring things by the next day; the person who buys trusts the promise and makes a plan for it; the things do not come. The promise is broken. But think of a person who promises a friend that he will go to the big party of the friend. The friend, who has known the ways of the person for a long time, does not trust the promise at all. He does not save a seat for the person, does not count the person as a person who comes, and does not make any plan that needs the person to be there. The friend is sure, in a quiet way, that the person will not come. And the person does not come. The first part is true – the words were said. The last part is true – the act was not done. But the next part is not true, because the other person did not trust the promise. Still it feels clear that the person has broken his promise. His not going is a break of his word even if the friend did not expect him to keep it. The idea added a part – trust – that comes from the law of promises rather than from the idea of right and wrong about promises, where the need is on the act of giving a word and is broken by not doing the act alone.
definition: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
counterexample: Suppose that a man promises his friend that he will attend the friend's wedding. The friend, knowing the man to be unreliable, places no reliance whatever upon the promise and makes no arrangements that depend upon his attendance. The man fails to attend. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second fails: the promisee did not rely upon the assurance. Yet it appears that the man has broken his promise. A promise is broken by non-performance, not by the disappointment of reliance; the friend's scepticism does not dissolve the obligation.
missing_condition: The definition makes the promisee's reliance a necessary element of promise-breaking, but a promise may be broken even where the promisee placed no reliance upon it; the obligation inheres in the act of promising itself, not in the expectations it happens to generate.

question: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
expected_answer:
Suppose a person says a count number we are looking at is a first kind of number only if the number we are looking at is more than one, the number we are looking at cannot be made by putting two count numbers each more than one together, and the thing we get when we take away one from the number we are looking at cannot be cut into parts that are the same.  

The first two needs are clear: the number must be more than one, and it must not be broken into smaller parts. These are the usual needs.  

The third need, however, needs a close look.  

Think about any count number that is more than one. The thing we get when we take away one from that number is always less than that number, and a smaller count number is never able to be cut into parts that are the same as a bigger one.  

So the thing we get when we take away one from that number cannot be cut into parts that are the same as that number for every count number that is more than one, no matter if it is a first kind of number or made of smaller parts.  

The need is very easily true and does not leave out any things at all.  

It looks like it puts a stop, but it does not.  

Someone might see that a good explain that has a need that does nothing is broken not because it gives wrong answers — it does not — but because it shows the form of the idea wrong when it tries to look at it.  

A not careful reader may think that the third need does real work, when in fact the whole hard work is on the first two needs.  

A good explain of first kind of number should have only needs that each are needed and together enough, with no extra words that only make it look hard.
definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
counterexample: Every prime number satisfies the condition that n minus one is not divisible by n, but so does every composite number greater than one, since n minus one is always strictly less than n and hence never divisible by n. The third condition is vacuous; it excludes nothing and adds nothing to the definition. Meanwhile, the first two conditions alone already characterise the primes, making the third condition wholly redundant rather than genuinely restrictive.
missing_condition: The third condition is trivially satisfied by every natural number greater than one and therefore does no work in the definition. A proper characterisation should contain no vacuous clauses, and the essence of primality is fully captured by the first two conditions alone.

question: counterexample to: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts in the face of danger, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary.
expected_answer: Some people might say that the feeling to act when there is fear is a set way of being that comes from a choice to act when there is a thing that can hurt. This idea shows the many times it happens, the set way, and the no force that many people often bring together a person who has that feeling. But a situation that shows this is not enough can be taken from the place where work people do things that can hurt. Imagine a person who makes a work life of jumping from houses on fire, driving cars very fast, and falling from high places. She does this many times, from a way of being made by many years of learning, and she acts by her own choice — no one forces her to do it. All three parts are true. Still, we should be careful to call this good part the feeling to act when there is fear in its right idea. Her want to meet a thing that can hurt is not led by any worry about what is really good enough for getting hurt; she looks for a thing that can hurt for money and for the sound of hands of people who watch, and she does not care at all if the thing that can hurt she meets helps any important end. Some people might see that the meaning, when it looks just at the many times, the set way, and the own choice of a person who acts when there is a thing that can hurt, cannot tell the real person with the feeling to act when there is fear different from the work person who does things that can hurt whose way of being, even if set, is led by thoughts that have no good reason.
definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts in the face of danger, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary.
counterexample: Consider a professional stunt performer who regularly faces physical danger, does so from a settled occupational disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and acts entirely voluntarily. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the stunt performer may be wholly indifferent to the moral significance of the dangers she faces; she courts risk for pay and spectacle, not because she recognises that something of genuine value is at stake. We should hesitate to call her courageous in the full moral sense, for her disposition is mercenary rather than responsive to considerations of worth.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's disposition to face danger be governed by a correct apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking harm for — that the agent face danger because and only because something of sufficient moral importance demands it.

question: counterexample to: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
expected_answer:
One person might say a good enough act of changing words from one language to another is a thing that keeps the true meaning, follows the ways that words are put together, and uses words that are the same as the first words.  

This idea shows the things we want: the right meaning, easy to read, and close enough.  

But a case that shows it is not enough comes from a well known thing of places where there are no words between languages.  

Think about a word from a language that many people in a part of the world speak that means a special kind of warm feeling, nice feeling, and a feeling of being at home.  

There is no one word in the language that many people in a part of the world speak that is the same as it.  

A good enough act of changing words from one language to another for a set of words that has that word has to use many words or a different way of saying – maybe “a warm and nice feeling” – but no one word in the language that many people in a part of the world speak is the same as that word.  

The one after the second way, which needs words that are the same, does not happen.  

Still the many word act of changing words from one language to another can be good enough: it keeps the true meaning, it follows the ways that words are put together, and it gives the idea of the first set of words with close enough.  

The idea, by saying we must have words that are the same, makes a part that cannot happen when languages cut the world in not the same ways with their words at the part.  

What we need is a less hard part – maybe that the act of changing words from one language to another just gives the idea of the first set of words using any words we have, without needing each word to have the same word.
definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
counterexample: Consider the French sentence 'Il pleut.' A truth-conditionally adequate translation into English is 'It is raining.' The truth-conditions are preserved, the English sentence is grammatical, and the vocabulary corresponds. Now consider the German sentence 'Gemutlichkeit herrscht im Raum.' The truth-conditions might be preserved by the English sentence 'There is a cosy, warm, convivial atmosphere in the room.' But the single German word 'Gemutlichkeit' has no single English equivalent; the translation requires a circumlocution. The third condition — vocabulary correspondence — is not met, yet the translation may be perfectly adequate.
missing_condition: The definition requires vocabulary correspondence, but fails to recognise that adequate translation may require circumlocution, paraphrase, or the use of expressions that lack any one-to-one correspondence with the source vocabulary.

question: counterexample to: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
expected_answer: one might say that a thing that have to be true is a thing that is true, that saying it is not true leads to something that can not be true, and that we can know it just by thinking. this talk has an old story: a group of people who think about reason from old person who thinks to people who think that only things we can see are real said that have to be true, things that are true just by how they are said, and things we can know just by thinking were all the same. a case that shows this thought is not enough can come from how we talk about words. think about the thought that the star we see in the sky after the day is the same star we see in the sky early in the day — that the bright thing we see in the sky after the day is the very same thing we see in the sky early in the day. this thought is true. also, because both names point to the same thing, saying the thought is not true would be saying that the thing is not the thing, which can not be true. so the thought meets the first two things. but it does not meet the thing after the second: finding out that the star we see after the day and the star we see early in the day are the same thing needed looking at the sky with things we use. no lot of just thinking could have shown they are the same. it seems that the talk that says we have to be able to know something just by thinking, as a have to be true thing, can not fit ideas that are have to be true because the two names point to the same thing, not because of the way the thought is said.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
counterexample: Consider the proposition that Hesperus is Phosphorus — that is, that the evening star is the morning star. This proposition is true. Given that both names rigidly designate the planet Venus, its denial leads to a contradiction: it would assert that Venus is not Venus. Yet the proposition is not a priori knowable: it required empirical astronomical observation to discover that the two celestial appearances are one and the same object. All three conditions cannot simultaneously be satisfied, and yet the proposition appears to be necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for necessary truths that are a posteriori — that is, truths whose necessity arises from the identities of the objects involved rather than from the logical form of the proposition, and which therefore require empirical investigation to discover.

question: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
expected_answer:
A simple story that works says it gives a reason that is good enough for right and wrong for why a kind god lets bad things happen, and that this reason fits with god being all power and all kind, and that it talks about how many bad things and where they are in the world. The way it says catches the thinking and right and wrong needs that any put together must meet. 

A thing that shows it is not enough comes with the story about why a kind god lets bad because people can choose. This story says that real people can choose – which is a big good – needs the chance that they might use it in a wrong way, and a world with people who can choose is more good than a world of only things that work by themselves. The reason given is good for right and wrong and fits with the parts of the god. 

One might see that this story only talks about bad things that come from people choosing on a plan. It does not talk about bad things that happen from the earth shaking, big hurt that kills many people, bad hurt inside a child who did not make a choice. How many and where those bad things are still not talked about at all. 

The way it says it must talk about the seen bad things, but does not say it must talk about every kind of bad thing, lets a story that explains one kind of bad thing while leaving another kind not known.
definition: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
counterexample: Consider the free-will theodicy, which holds that a benevolent deity permits evil because the possession of genuine free will requires the possibility of its misuse, and a world with free agents is more valuable than one without. This provides a morally sufficient reason consistent with omnipotence and benevolence. Yet it does not obviously account for natural evils — earthquakes, plagues, childhood cancers — which are not the result of any agent's free choice. The theodicy addresses moral evil but leaves natural evil unexplained.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T account for every category of evil — both moral and natural — so that a theodicy which addresses one source of evil while leaving another entirely unexplained may count as successful.

question: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.
expected_answer:
Think about three things that always go without big changes.  

The first thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives the number times itself. It goes without big changes at every point on the line of true numbers. It works everywhere; for every line of numbers that gets closer to a given point, the new line of numbers also gets closer to the number times itself of that point; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The second thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that gets bigger fast when the number that goes in gets bigger. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The third thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that goes up and down in the same way. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it itself stays not too big, so it also turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big.  

From these cases we can make a thought: a thing that changes a number into another number is without big changes at a point if and only if it works there, it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to that point the new line also gets closer, and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in every small area around the point.  

The first two thoughts are the well known way of saying without big changes with lines of numbers, which is the same as the usual small number thought and everyone says it is right.  

The third thought – that the thing turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in small areas around the point – is true for all three case things and might look like a different needed thought. Yet it is not different. It comes from the line thought. If a thing keeps the idea that every line of numbers that gets closer to a point also gets closer after the thing, then it stays not too big in every small area around that point, and so it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big in that area into sets of numbers that stay not too big. The third thought adds no new thought that is already in the second. The case things were all good things that showed many nice things beyond just being without big changes, and the thought has taken one such nice thing – staying not too big – and raised it up as a different thought, when in truth it follows from the line thought as a clear end. The look at this has taken true words about without big changes things for a different thought, making the thought look more strong without adding any real new thought.
definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.
counterexample: Consider the function f(x) = x^2 on the entire real line. This function is continuous everywhere by any standard criterion. Now consider a bounded set, say the closed interval [0, n], and the function g(x) = x^2. The image of [0, n] under g is [0, n^2], which is bounded. So for polynomial functions the third condition tends to be satisfied on bounded sets. But consider instead the function h(x) = x, the identity function, which is continuous everywhere. It trivially satisfies the third condition. The difficulty arises when one considers the converse direction: a function that satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third. Consider f(x) = 1/x for x not equal to 0, with f(0) = 0. This function is continuous at every point except 0 in the standard sense. At the point 0 it fails the second condition (and so is not continuous there), but this means the third condition adds nothing to the analysis at points of continuity. For all functions continuous at a point, the third condition is automatically satisfied. The third condition is redundant, adding no content beyond what the first two conditions already provide.

question: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
expected_answer:
One may say that real past change is made of more good things for people, more ways to act for people, and more ways to make and share ideas. This way of talking shows the money, the way people are told what to do, and the thinking parts that we most often join with the idea of change.  

But a story that shows this not enough can be taken from the past of big machine life.  

Imagine a group of people, over a long time of many years, see a big rise in the good life for most of them: food is more, homes are safer, and sick is less. At the same time, the number of ways to act grows: the chance to choose who leads is given to more, things that stop what people say are less, and laws that keep people safe are stronger. And the ways of the group to make and share ideas get very big because of new schools, the making of pages that are made by pressing letters, and the start of rooms with books that anyone can use. All three parts of the idea are true.  

But we should not say this change is all good, because at the same time the group makes big machine ways that hurt the earth in ways that may not be able to be fixed. The good things in life, ways to act, and ideas come at the thing we pay for hurting the earth that puts at the chance of the staying of those good things.  

It looks like the idea, by only asking for more good life, more ways to act, and more ideas, does not ask that these more things can stay and not be taken away from the children that come later. If we do not have that ask, a group may meet every part of change while slowly breaking the place of its own staying good.
definition: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
counterexample: Suppose a society over the course of two centuries experiences a dramatic rise in the material standard of living for the majority of its members, a significant expansion of civil liberties, and a vast increase in its capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge through universities, printing, and public libraries. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet during the same period, the society develops industrial capacities that cause severe and potentially irreversible damage to the natural environment upon which future generations depend. The gains in material welfare, liberty, and knowledge are purchased at the cost of ecological degradation that threatens the sustainability of those very gains.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the improvements be sustainable and not purchased at the cost of harms to future generations or to the natural conditions upon which continued human flourishing depends, so that a society may satisfy all conditions of progress while undermining the foundations of its own future welfare.

question: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
expected_answer: One might say that a person can be said to have a must do thing when the person had a must do thing, the bad thing would not have happened if the must do thing had been done, and the not doing was the only thing that made the bad thing happen. The first two parts show the main idea of a must do thing for not doing: there is a must do thing and the bad thing would change if the must do thing was not done. The third part, saying it must be the only thing that made the bad thing happen, might look like it makes us point to the right person. But this part is too hard. Think of a person who works to watch the water at a water place and does not save a person who is going down in the water. The person who watches the water had a must do thing, and the person in the water would have lived if the person who watches the water had stepped in. But the bad thing had many helping things: the place had a not seen far down water part, another worker did not put up a sign that says bad, and a person who was there and could have thrown a thing that keeps a person up did not act. The not doing of the person who watches the water was not the only thing – it was one of many things happening at the same time. The third part does not work. Still no group of people who decide would clear the person who watches the water just because other things also helped the person go down. The law often says a person can be said to have a must do thing for not doing even when that not doing is needed but not enough by itself, because other things also help. The idea that asks for only one cause would end up keeping safe any person whose not doing happened together with other helping things – an end that would take away most of the law power for not doing.
definition: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
counterexample: Consider a lifeguard who fails to rescue a drowning swimmer. The lifeguard had a duty to act, and the swimmer would not have drowned had the lifeguard intervened. However, another bystander also had the opportunity to help and did nothing, and the pool's design — with an unmarked deep end — also contributed to the drowning. The lifeguard's omission was not the sole cause of the harm; there were concurrent contributing causes. The third condition is not met, yet the lifeguard is plainly liable for the omission.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement that the omission be the sole cause is too stringent; the law routinely holds persons liable for omissions that are concurrent causes of harm alongside other contributing factors.

question: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
expected_answer: Many people think that a piece of talk says an added idea when the truth of that idea is taken as true in the talk, and when the other piece of talk also takes it as true, and if the added idea is not true then the piece of talk is not true and not not true. The one after the second part gives the idea its strong point: it shows the added idea is not the same as a simple idea by putting it with places where there is no true or not true answer. But that part is not something that everyone agrees on. Look at the words “the person who leads the land we call has no hair on the head.” Both these words and the other words take it as true that the land we call has a person who leads; the first part and the second part are met. But if the land we call does not have a person who leads, is the piece of talk without a true or not true answer, or is it just not true? One well known idea says it is not true, calling the saying that there is a person who leads as not an added idea but as part of what the words say. In this way the one after the second part does not work, but we still want to say that the piece of talk takes it as true that there is a person who leads the land we call – the talk would be bad and have no point if no person who leads was. The meaning, it seems, has put together a real world thing – the parts for when a talk is good – with a meaning thing – the parts for when it has a true or not true answer – and these can be different.
definition: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The king of France is bald.' Its negation, 'The king of France is not bald,' equally takes for granted that there is a king of France. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet one might argue, as Russell did, that the sentence is straightforwardly false when France has no king, rather than truth-valueless. The third condition fails on the Russellian analysis, though we should still wish to say that the sentence presupposes the existence of a king of France.
missing_condition: Presupposition may be a pragmatic phenomenon — a condition on the felicity of utterance — rather than a semantic one governing truth-value, and the definition conflates these two levels.

question: counterexample to: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics that are shared by the events and developments within it, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study.
expected_answer:
One might say that a real way to cut time into parts shows each time by things that are the same and also things that are different, makes lines at big changes, and can be used for the whole area we look at. This picture shows that the parts fit together inside and can be used outside, like good ways to cut time into parts do.  

But one case that shows this not enough can come from the story of the west. The usual cut between the old time and the new time puts the line at about the year 1400. In the towns in the west, this line shows real changes: the new start of old school learning, the coming of new art ways, the growing of buying and selling and money making. This way to cut time into parts meets all three parts when used for the towns in the west.  

But in the north lands, the ways people live, the ways money works, and the ways they make art that belong to the old time kept going far into the year 1500. The line at year 1400, even if it shows a big change in one area, tells a wrong story about how things change in another area.  

One can see that the idea, by saying it only has to work for the whole area and not saying it must fit the different times of change in each part, lets a plan made for one place be used on places where it does not fit the time.
definition: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics that are shared by the events and developments within it, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study.
counterexample: Consider the standard periodisation of European history that places the boundary between the Middle Ages and the Renaissance at approximately 1400. In Italian city-states, this boundary corresponds to genuine cultural and economic transitions. But in Scandinavia, the social and cultural patterns characteristic of the medieval period persisted well into the sixteenth century. The periodisation meets all three conditions when applied to Italy but fails to capture the actual rhythm of historical change across the whole of Europe.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the periodisation accommodate regional variation in the timing of the transitions it identifies, so that a scheme which accurately marks ruptures in one region but misrepresents the pace of change in others may be applied uncritically to the entire geographic scope.

question: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
expected_answer:
One might say an always true thing is a thing that is true in all possible times, its truth can be shown by looking at its way the words are put together alone, and that can be said using only the usual words that make true by the way the words are put together.  

This meaning fits the first set of always true things — the first thing or not the first thing, and if the first thing and the second thing then the first thing — with very clear words.  

These things are true no matter what the first thing and the second thing are, and their truth can be seen from their way the words are put together.  

But one case that shows the not enough of this look is given by things whose must be true comes not from the way the words are put together but from the meaning of their words.  

The thing “If something is the first kind then it has a look” is true in every possible time: nothing can be the first kind and not have a look.  

Its truth is as sure as any other always true thing that is true by the way the words are put together.  

But it cannot be shown to be true by looking at its way the words are put together, because its way — “If something is the first thing then something is the second thing” — is not an always true thing that is true by the way the words are put together (there are many things of this set that are not true).  

Its must be true rests on the meaning that go together between the first kind and the look, a meaning that the look at the way the words are put together cannot get.  

One might see that the meaning has been made to fit the things we use for thinking about these things and leaves out must be true things that need the form of the words or on meaning that go together between words — an end that shows the ends of a certain set of ways rather than the ends of the idea of an always true thing.
definition: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'If something is red, then it is coloured.' This proposition is true in all possible circumstances — nothing could be red without being coloured. Yet its truth cannot be established by examining its logical form alone; it has the form 'If Fx then Gx,' which is not a truth-functional tautology. Its truth depends upon the meanings of 'red' and 'coloured,' not upon the arrangement of connectives. Furthermore, it involves predicate structure rather than being expressible using only truth-functional connectives. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not met, yet the proposition is necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tautology to truth-functional form, thereby excluding necessary truths whose necessity arises from the meanings of their constituent terms or from the structure of predicate logic rather than from truth-functional logic.

question: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, (ii) S has a characteristic set of physical and chemical properties — a definite melting point, density, reactivity, and so forth — that distinguish it from all other elements, and (iii) S exists as a single, uniform phase under standard conditions — that is, S is homogeneous.
expected_answer:
Think about three things that we call a kind of single part thing.  

All the tiny parts of the first thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is soft, yellow, metal, does not get wear away easily, it is made only of tiny parts that all have the number 79, and it has a set point where it turns to a hot soft form, a way it is heavy for its size, and a way it likes to change with other things that make it different from any other thing.  

All the tiny parts of the second thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is hard, dark, metal, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 26, and it also has its own set of things it does.  

All the tiny parts of the third thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is an air like thing with no color and no smell that is needed for breathing, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 8.  

Each thing is made of tiny parts of one kind, has its own set of things it does, and is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

We could write a thought: a thing is a single part thing if it is made of tiny parts that all have the same number, it has its own set of things it does, and it is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

Now think about the thing with the number 6. Any person who study things would call it a single part thing. Yet the thing does not have just one set of things it does.  

The hard clear form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together so each tiny part is put together with four others in a shape that goes in three ways – is the hardest thing we know, clear, very bright, and it does not let force move, and it needs a very high hot point where it turns to a hot soft form.  

The soft dark form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together in flat sheets that look like many little flat pieces – is one of the softest hard things we know, not clear, dark, and so good at letting force move that we use it in pieces that help move force.  

The two forms have different ways they are heavy for their size, different ways the tiny parts are put together, different ways they look with light, and different ways they let force move.  

When we look at them with our eyes, they are as different as two things can be.  

In the usual way we see it, the thing with number 6 is not all the same way all over; it can be the hard clear form or it can be the soft dark form, and both are okay to call it the thing with number 6.  

The second and third parts of the thought are not true.  

The earlier cases were things that happen to be only one usual form – one form of the thing with number 79, one form of the thing with number 26, one form of the thing with number 8 – and the thought took this same as needed.  

The thing with number 6 shows that what makes a thing a single part thing is not the way it looks or acts on a big size but the fact that its tiny parts are the same, and that one single part thing can look very different on how its tiny parts are put together.
definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, (ii) S has a characteristic set of physical and chemical properties — a definite melting point, density, reactivity, and so forth — that distinguish it from all other elements, and (iii) S exists as a single, uniform phase under standard conditions — that is, S is homogeneous.
counterexample: Consider carbon. Carbon is composed entirely of atoms with atomic number six, and it is classified as a chemical element without controversy. But carbon does not have a single characteristic set of physical properties. As diamond, it is the hardest known natural substance, transparent, and an electrical insulator. As graphite, it is one of the softest solids, opaque, black, and an electrical conductor. Diamond and graphite have different melting points, different densities, and different crystal structures. Under standard conditions, carbon does not exist as a single, uniform phase; it may exist as diamond or as graphite. The third condition fails, and the second is troubled, for there is no single set of properties that characterises carbon.

question: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
expected_answer: Someone could say that a time is in the past only when it has happened, it is not now, and it is kept in the memory of at least one mind that felt it. That idea has a kind of easy feeling. When we think about things that happened before — a day when we were small and had food, a walk in the morning on a day before, a paper that people signed — we think about things that have happened, that are not happening now, and that are kept in the memory of the people who saw them. Memory looks like the way the past stays alive; without it, someone could think the past would be nothing — just an empty space where something used to be. But we must think about how the earth was made. That thing happened many many years ago, far before any mind was there. Hot rock came together from a cloud of dust and air; the outside got cold; the first hard outside part became hard over many years. These things have happened and are not now. They are, by any good story, in the past. But no mind saw them; no memory keeps them. We guess they happened from the rock story — from what rocks are made of, from the way rock parts change over time, from space rocks — but this guess is not memory. Memory needs a mind that was there at the thing and that keeps a thought of it, and there was no mind like that when the earth was made. The third part of that idea does not work. The idea has joined together the way we most often understand the past — memory — with what makes something past. A time does not need a person who saw it to have happened and to be not now; the past goes far beyond what any mind can touch, and most of what has happened happened without any mind to see it.
definition: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
counterexample: Consider the formation of the earth approximately four and a half thousand million years ago. This event occurred; it is no longer present; and it is unquestionably in the past. Yet no conscious being experienced it, and consequently it is not preserved in any being's memory. There were no observers — no minds of any sort — when the molten rock condensed and the first crust formed. The third condition fails entirely, yet the event is as firmly in the past as any event could be.
missing_condition: The definition ties the pastness of a moment to its preservation in some conscious being's memory, but the past extends far beyond the reach of any consciousness. What is wanted is an account of pastness that does not make the existence of the past dependent upon the contingent existence of minds.

question: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
expected_answer:
The acts of naming that we most easy see as good share a shape that simple talks often miss.  

When parents give a child a name at a baby naming day, the good of the naming needs not only the saying of a word that tries to point to the child, but also the people group and ideas that run the thing where the saying happens — a special day where people gather, a paper that writes down when a baby is born, people seeing the name.  

When a person who studies green things gives a new kind of green thing a two word name, the name works because it follows the set of ideas that tell what to do, the world wide set of name ideas, which gives power and makes it stay on the name.  

When a country gives a big bridge a name at a special day where people gather to give a name, the good needs the power given to the person who does the naming and the people group and ideas that run the work of many people.  

One may try to say this by calling good naming a saying made with the trying to point, with the thing being seen, by a person who says and knows about giving names.  

Yet a case that shows this idea is not enough can be taken from child play.  

Suppose a child, alone in a garden, picks up a different stone and says very serious, “I name you the name we will call the stone.”  

The child says the name with real trying to point.  

The child holds the stone and is seeing it.  

The child knows what giving a name is, because they have watched baby naming days and giving names to animals that live with people.  

Every part is met.  

Yet if no other person ever learns of or knows this name, the name has no good for other people, no power to make people follow, and does not stay beyond the child own mind.  

One can see that what the other cases share – and what the short saying does not need – is that the act of giving a name be put inside or accepted by a known people group and ideas that run the thing, so that the name has power to make people follow and can be shared, known, and kept by other people.
definition: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
counterexample: Suppose a child, playing alone in a garden, picks up an unusual stone and declares, 'I name you Rupert.' The child utters the name with the genuine intention of designating the stone. The child is in perceptual contact with the stone. And the child possesses the concept of naming — the child understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation, having observed christenings and the naming of pets. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet if no one else ever learns of or recognises this designation, the name 'Rupert' for this particular stone has no social currency, no normative force, and no permanence beyond the child's own memory. We should hesitate to regard this as a successful act of naming in the full sense.

question: counterexample to: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent conditions necessary for E, (ii) A subsumes E under a general regularity or pattern, and (iii) A is consistent with the surviving documentary evidence.
expected_answer:
The best clear stories about the past all have a big part that we can look at. When the first writer told about the big war in an old place, he said there was a big reason — the rise of a city and the fear it made — and also the small reasons that started the fighting. When the writer who told about a big change in 1789 told the story, he put the things that happened in 1789 inside a long change of the people there, but he did not say they were only a simple idea. When the writer who told about the fall of a sea area told the story, he put together the edges of the land, the ways money moved, and the small things run by the people who run the place over many times. In each story, the telling worked because it put together the big picture and the small picture — by showing how big reasons made the small things happen but did not decide all.

Someone could try to make a simple idea for a good story about the past by saying it must name the things that had to be there first, put the thing inside a big way, and fit what the old papers say. But a story about the fall of a big city in 1453 shows that this simple idea is not enough. We can name the things that had to be there — the old group of people who fight got less strong, a new group got bigger, and help from the west did not come — and we can put the fall inside the big way that big groups whose groups of people who fight get less strong while a close group gets bigger can be taken over. The story fits all the old papers. All three things are there. But the story does not tell why the fall happened in the way it did: the leader used many big things that can hurt that had never been used before, a lucky find of a door that was left open, and small choices of the people who tried to keep safe. These small things were needed for how the fall really was. Someone can see that other stories show, and the simple idea does not get, that a story about the past must put together the big picture and the small picture, by putting big form reasons to the small things that make each past event have its own shape.
definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent conditions necessary for E, (ii) A subsumes E under a general regularity or pattern, and (iii) A is consistent with the surviving documentary evidence.
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the fall of Constantinople in 1453 that cites the necessary antecedent conditions — the weakening of Byzantine military capacity, the rise of Ottoman power, the failure of Western reinforcements — subsumes the event under the general regularity that empires whose military capacity has declined relative to an expanding neighbour are liable to conquest, and is consistent with all surviving documents. All three conditions are met. Yet the explanation treats the fall as an instance of a general pattern and cannot account for the specific form the event took — Sultan Mehmed's innovative use of massive artillery, the chance discovery of an unlocked gate in the walls, the particular decisions of the defenders. These contingent details, while not subsumable under any general regularity, were essential to the actual course of events.

question: counterexample to: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) any two points in X can be joined by a continuous path lying entirely within X, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the integers is constant.
expected_answer:
The places that most people see first when they start to look at shapes have a thing that can make a person think it is a must for being joined together. The closed short line between two numbers, the open flat round shape, and the ball shape are all joined together in the way we think about places, and in each case any two things can be joined by a way that does not break and stays inside the place. Every thing that does not break and goes from these places to whole numbers always gives the same whole number. These notes ask for a big talk: a place is joined together when you can not break it into two parts that both have at least one thing and do not share any things and are open, when any two things can be joined by a way that does not break, and when every thing that does not break and goes to whole numbers always gives the same whole number.  

But a story that shows this idea is not enough can be taken from the look at a part of a flat big place that acts in a strange way near its edge. Look at the whole thing that has the picture of a line that goes up and down a lot when the first thing gets close to the number that is nothing for the first thing in the part between the number that is nothing and one. This place has a line that goes up and down a lot together with an up and down line at the first thing is the number that is nothing. You can not break it into two parts that both have at least one thing and do not share any things and are open; it is joined together. Every thing that does not break and goes from it to whole numbers always gives the same whole number. The first idea and the third idea are true. But no way that does not break can join any thing on the up and down line to any thing on the line that goes up and down a lot, because such a way would have to go through many up and down moves in a short time. The second idea is not true. What the earlier stories have the same and why we added the way idea is that short lines between numbers, flat round shapes, and ball shapes are not just joined together but can be joined by a way that does not break. The talk, by making the way idea a must, puts together two different ideas about places and leaves out places that are really joined together in the way the first and third ideas say.
definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) any two points in X can be joined by a continuous path lying entirely within X, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the integers is constant.
counterexample: Consider the topologist's sine curve: the closure in the plane of the graph of sin(1/x) for x in (0, 1]. This space consists of the oscillating curve together with the vertical segment from (0, -1) to (0, 1). It cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets — it is connected in the topological sense. Every continuous function from it to the integers is constant, since a connected space admits only constant maps into a discrete space. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet condition (ii) fails: there is no continuous path from any point on the vertical segment to any point on the oscillating curve, because any such path would need to traverse infinitely many oscillations. The space is connected but not path-connected.

question: counterexample to: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they moderate their appetites, (ii) they do so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) they experience no conflict in doing so.
expected_answer:
One can say this way of not having too much like this: a person has the way of not having too much if and only if he controls his wants, he does this from a not changing feeling instead of from a short try, and he feels no fight inside when he does it.  

The third part is meant to tell the two as not the same the really not having too much person from the person who only stops wants by a strong will — the person who only uses his will to stop wants.  

The really not having too much person, in this way, feels no pull to having too much.  

This saying catches the old good person best idea in a good way.  

But it leaves out a person that we should not want to not give the name of not having too much.  

Think about a person who for many years liked to have too much drink, and who by long hard work and control of his own act got a not changing feeling of not drinking.  

He controls his want in a not changing way and from a not changing part of the person; the first two parts are met.  

But he still feels a pull.  

The want for drink has not gone; it stays as a small but real fight inside him.  

The third part does not work.  

But one can see that his not having too much is more good because it stays even though this small want stays.  

To not give him the name of not having too much because he still feels the pull of want is to keep the good thing only for people who are lucky in how they are made and to leave out people who got it by hard good work.
definition: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they moderate their appetites, (ii) they do so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) they experience no conflict in doing so.
counterexample: A recovering drunkard who has, through years of discipline, achieved a settled disposition of sobriety moderates his appetite for drink from a stable character trait. Yet he continues to experience temptation — a quiet but persistent conflict between desire and resolution. The third condition fails. But we should not hesitate to call such a man temperate; indeed, his temperance is the more admirable for being maintained in the face of ongoing temptation.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the temperate man experience no conflict, but temperance may coexist with residual desire; what is essential is that the agent reliably governs their appetites, not that they have altogether extinguished them.

question: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
expected_answer:
Consider three case where a person gets a right to ground by long use. A person who grows food marks the edge of a piece of empty ground, turns over the ground, and uses it open for twenty years; at the end of that time people call him the one who has the ground. A person who lives in an empty house, makes it better, and lives there open and all the time for twenty years; people call his right true. A person who works on a boat uses a piece of the side of a river, ties his boat there and makes his catching things dry there, all open and without hide or fight for twenty years; people call his right true. In each case the holding is all the time, open, and against the true one who has the ground okay – that is, without the true one who has the ground okay. From these case one can make an idea: a person gets a right by long use if and only if his holding is all the time for the needed time, open and well known, and against the true one who has the ground wishes.

But one must think about a harder case. Suppose a person lives on a piece of ground next to his own ground because he really but wrong thought it is part of the ground he has. A line that shows the side was drawn wrong on the big paper, and the person, using the paper, uses the piece as his own. He marks the edge, grows things on it, and keeps it for the needed time. His holding is all the time and not stopped. It is open and well known; all the close people can see that he uses the piece. And it is against the true one who has the ground because he does not have the true one who has the ground okay; the true one who has the ground lives far away and does not know about the taking. The three parts of the idea seem to be met. Yet the kind of his holding is different from the person who grows food and knew he was taking another person empty ground. The person who grows food used the ground as his own even though he knew it was not; his holding was a say against the true one who has the ground. The person who uses the wrong paper also uses the ground as his own, but without any thought that it is against the true one who has the ground; he does not know he is taking. The earlier case were all times when the person knew he was saying another person empty ground – times when the say was both without okay and the person used the ground as his own. The idea said the no okay but did not say the person also must use the ground as his own – a part that is different from just the no okay and that the idea says is needed for the time of the long use idea.
definition: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
counterexample: Consider a tenant who occupies a house under a lease for twenty-one years. His possession is continuous and uninterrupted; it is open and notorious — the whole neighbourhood knows he lives there. Yet his possession is not adverse to the true owner's interests; he occupies the house with the owner's permission, under a lease, and pays rent. The three conditions of the definition, taken at face value, are satisfied with respect to continuity and openness, but the third condition — adversity — fails. However, one may construct a more troubling case. Suppose a person occupies land in the genuine but mistaken belief that he is the true owner — because, for example, a boundary has been incorrectly drawn upon a map. His possession is continuous, open, and adverse in the sense that he does not have permission from the true owner. Yet he does not regard his possession as adverse; he believes himself to be the owner. The definition, which speaks of possession 'adverse to the interests of the true owner,' captures this case only if adversity is understood objectively — as the absence of permission — rather than subjectively, as a hostile intent. The definition has omitted the requirement that the possessor must treat the land as his own, which is a further condition not reducible to the mere absence of permission.

question: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.
expected_answer: The normal story of give back says that a person can get back from another person who has got more things at the first person thing they paid, if there is no law reason for keeping it and the person who asks has had a hurt. This way says the main idea of the law about not just getting more things: that no person should get a good thing at another person thing they paid without a law reason. Yet a story that shows this idea is not enough can be thought of without trouble. Think of a person who makes a building, because of a wrong in a place name, builds a big more part on the wrong house, making the house of the person who lives next door better instead of the house of the person who asked. The house of the person who lives next door is now more – she got more things. There is no paper note or other law reason for her to keep the better part – no reason is there. The person who makes a building has used work and stuff – he has had a hurt. All three ideas of the story are met. Yet the person who lives next door did not ask for or want the more part. It is a look she does not think is nice, and she would have said no if she had been asked. To make her give money for a part she did not want – a part she could not say no to – looks like not just. It seems that the story, by looking at more things, no law reason, and hurt, does not think about if the more things were taken by own will. If we do not have that idea, the law about give back could force people to give money for good things they never looked for and do not like.
definition: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.
counterexample: Consider a builder who, through a clerical error, constructs an extension on the wrong property, improving a neighbour's house rather than the client's. The neighbour is enriched — her property has increased in value. There is no contract or other legal basis for her retention of the improvement. And the builder has suffered a corresponding loss — the expenditure of labour and materials. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the neighbour neither requested nor desired the improvement, and the extension is of a style that diminishes her enjoyment of the property. Requiring her to pay for an unwanted improvement seems itself unjust.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to consider whether the enrichment was voluntarily accepted or requested by T — that restitution should not compel a person to pay for a benefit that was neither sought nor willingly received.

question: counterexample to: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.
expected_answer: One could say that a person must do a thing if he has taken it on by his own choice, another person has a true hope that he will do it, and not doing it would hurt that person. This shows the must that come from promise and promise work. A person who has agreed to make a house has taken the work by his own choice, the other person has a true hope that the house will be made, and not making it would hurt the other person. All three parts are met, and the must is clear. But a story that shows the not enough of this idea is the must to help. Think of a man walking by low water who sees a young child fall into water and start to go down. He has not made a promise to help the child. He does not know the child or the child family. He has not taken any help work by his own choice. No person has a true hope that he will help – the meeting is all by chance. All three parts of the idea do not work. But the must to go into the water and pull the child out is, for most good feeling, very strong. The man who walks by, leaving the child to go down because he has not taken it on by his own choice to save it, has not done a must that feels as big as any promise must. One could note that the idea has made the must look like it comes from own choice promise and has not seen the must that come just from the situation you are in – a must that the situation puts on us not because we chose it but because it is there, and that hold us even if we did not say yes to be held.
definition: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.
counterexample: Consider a person who comes upon a child drowning in a shallow pond. He has not voluntarily undertaken to rescue the child; he has made no promise, signed no contract, assumed no role. No one has a legitimate expectation that this particular person will rescue this particular child — the encounter is entirely accidental. Yet most people would say without hesitation that he has an obligation to wade in and pull the child out, an obligation so strong that failure to act would be a moral enormity. All three conditions of the definition fail, yet the obligation is manifest.
missing_condition: The definition grounds obligation entirely in voluntary undertaking and the expectations of others, but there appear to be obligations — such as the obligation to rescue a person in immediate peril — that arise from the circumstances themselves, independently of any voluntary commitment or pre-existing expectation.

question: counterexample to: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups objects that share important properties, (ii) the groupings in C are stable under new observations — that is, newly discovered objects can be assigned to existing categories without restructuring the system, and (iii) C is consistent with the best available scientific theory.
expected_answer:
The best ways that people have put things into groups — a set of many small parts that are put in order by the way their inside parts are built, a way that a well known old person put living things into groups by the way their bodies look, a way that people put rocks into groups by the way their tiny parts are built — all have a same good point.  

Each one puts things together by parts about them that you cannot just see on the outside but that are part of the thing itself and that make other parts about the thing that people who study care about.  

The first set puts many small parts together by parts that come from the way their inside parts are built; the second way puts living things together by the way their bodies look, which shows they share a same family; the rock way uses the way the tiny parts are built, which decides how hard they are, how they break, and how they let light go through.  

From these big case we can try to make a way to put things into groups that is good for study only when it puts together things that share big important parts, when the groups stay the same when we see new things, and when the whole way fits with the best ideas we have.  

But this way, even though it works for the big case, also lets in ways to put things together that are not good for study.  

Think about a way to put stars into groups by how bright they look from our world — how much light we see.  

Stars that look about the same bright share a part that matters when we look at them; new stars we find can be given a bright look number without changing the groups; and this way of grouping fits well with the ideas we have about space, because the bright look number is a clear thing we can see.  

All three parts of the way are true.  

But this way of grouping does not tell us much about the stars, because it puts together stars that are really very different — a small close star that is not bright and a huge far star that is very bright can look the same bright — and it puts together stars that are really the same.  

The part we use to make the groups — how bright they look — is about where we are looking from, not about any real part of the stars.  

The big case worked because it used parts that belong to the things themselves, not because of where we look at them.  

The way says the parts we group by must be important, but it does not say they must be parts that belong to the things themselves and not to the way we see them.
definition: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups objects that share important properties, (ii) the groupings in C are stable under new observations — that is, newly discovered objects can be assigned to existing categories without restructuring the system, and (iii) C is consistent with the best available scientific theory.
counterexample: Consider a classification of stars based on their apparent brightness as seen from Earth — grouping together all stars of similar apparent magnitude. Stars of similar apparent brightness do share an 'important' property (they appear equally bright to an observer on Earth), the groupings are stable under new observations (newly discovered stars can readily be assigned an apparent magnitude), and the classification is consistent with astronomical theory (apparent magnitude is a well-defined observational quantity). All three conditions are met. Yet the classification groups together stars that are intrinsically very different — a dim nearby star and a luminous distant star may have the same apparent magnitude — and separates stars that are intrinsically similar. The classification is scientifically inadequate because apparent brightness reflects the observer's position rather than any property of the stars themselves.

question: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
expected_answer:
One might think that a group of numbers that you can add and see which is bigger in order is whole if and only if it has the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number, it has all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number inside it, and every number in the group can be written as one whole number over another whole number.  

The first two ideas make sense: the real numbers have the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number, and they do have all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number inside them.  

The third idea might seem to make sense if what you know about these groups of numbers is only the ones that can be written as one whole number over another whole number. Every such number is, by the words that say what it is, a part of one whole number over another whole number, and that group of numbers follows all three ideas.  

Yet one must think about the real numbers – the group that most people think of as the main whole group of numbers. The real numbers have the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number: for any number there is a whole number that is bigger. They have all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number.  

But not every real number is a part of one whole number over another whole number. The number that when you put it together with itself gives the number 2 is not a part of whole numbers, a fact that people have shown a long time ago and is one of the first and biggest things people have found in numbers. It cannot be written as one whole number over another whole number, no matter how in a good way you try to pick the top and bottom whole numbers.  

The third idea of the words that say what it is does not work for the real numbers.  

But the real numbers are whole; in fact, having the idea that any group of real numbers that has at least one number and has a biggest number also has a smallest biggest number inside the group is what makes them whole.  

The words that say what the numbers are only talked about the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number, not about the idea that makes a group whole, and so they put together groups that do not have the idea with groups that do.
definition: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
counterexample: Consider the real numbers. The real numbers form an ordered field that is Archimedean and contains the rational numbers as a subfield. Yet not every real number can be expressed as a ratio of two integers; the square root of 2, for instance, is irrational — a fact established by the ancient Greeks. The third condition fails, yet the field of real numbers is the paradigmatic example of a complete ordered field. The definition has confused completeness with the property of being the rational field.
missing_condition: The definition requires that every element be a ratio of integers, which characterises the rational field, not a complete ordered field. What is wanted is the condition that every non-empty subset with an upper bound has a least upper bound — the supremum property — which is the standard characterisation of completeness for ordered fields.

question: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
expected_answer: One might call a good talk about a big good thing as a talk that starts from thoughts that only reason can see, uses right steps and ends with the thought that a thing that has all the best things is real. This name shows the before any look step by step important part that we join with the most well known talks about the big good thing that come from looking at the world.
